1 Introduction In recent years, cloud-based applications have been developed with the support of various frameworks and technologies. Kubernetes and Docker are the mainstream invirtualizationtechnologywith84%usagein2023[1]. Dockerisanopen-source technology that encourages developers to build, deploy, run, update, and handle containers,andKubernetesisaportable,extensibleplatformforoperatingcontainerized applications[2,3]. Thesetechnologiessimplifythedeployment,increasethereusability andscalabilityofmicroservicessystems. Giventheirwidespreadadoption,therelated security concerns increase, for example [4]. Therefore, organizations such as OWASP and NSA research and provide their best practices to secure cloud deployments [5, 6]. The complex nature of cloud environments, which involve multiple abstraction layers and technologies, makes it harder to completely secure both applications and infrastructure. While there are tools and hardening guides available to protect Kubernetesclusters,thereisalackofacomprehensiveframework,leavingpractitioners the task of conveying all the fragmented knowledge into a list of actionable steps needed to harden the cluster and applications. The main objective of this thesis is to create a hardening framework based on previous state-of-the-art guidelines. This research combines the best existing methodstoproduceastrategytoreducethethreatexposureandlimitthenumberof vulnerabilities.

It groups common practices into a conceptual matrix that can be used to guide the developer’s efforts. To illustrate the practicality of the research, the framework is applied to a case studyonaKubernetescluster. Thecasestudyincludesthehardeningoftheclusterbut alsothesecuritystepsinvolvingtheapplicationanditscontainers. Theendresultis analyzed with a focus on the number of vulnerabilities mitigated. This thesis focuses on building the most comprehensive and complete security process for a typical Kubernetes cluster. The guideline covers most of the attack vectorsintheclusterwithfine-graineddetailthatteamscaneasilyfollowandapply to production environments. The research incorporates existing state-of-the-art approaches, tools, and configuration practices. Given the large number of guides and individualstepspresentintheliteraturereview,thisthesisleveragesthosewithamajor impact to form a new framework. The scope of this thesis is limited to Kubernetes and container technologies, focusingonDocker. TheresearchfocusesonsecuringageneralKubernetescluster and is cloud provider agnostic. A more complete solution would require an extension to cover specific cloud provider solutions and configurations.

The developed method takes into account the real-world software development process and makes it easy for teams in charge of one step of the life cycle to understand what security steps are needed. This thesis is organized as follows. Chapter 2 presents the background around the related topic. Chapter 3 reviews the current state of the art practices being used in Kubernetessecurity. Chapter4explainstheproposedmethodandclarifiesbrokendownitems. Chapter5examinesthecasestudy,evaluatingthemethodusingqualitative andquantitativeresults. Chapter6discussesthefeasibilityandadaptabilityofthenew

Section: Hardening Application Deployment in Kubernetes > Page 12. approachinsoftwaredevelopment,challengesapplyingthescientificprocesstothe casestudy,andthefuture workforimprovements. Finally,chapter7summarizesthe work and draws a conclusion from the process.

2 Background This chapter covers basic concepts and technologies related to containers and their orchestration. Section2.1coversthebasicinformationaboutcontainersandDocker. Section 2.2 describes Kubernetes, the most used technology to orchestrate containers, andthefocusofthiswork. Section2.3definesDevOps,amodernsoftwaredevelopment approach,whichistheideaforthelaterproposedmethod. Finally,section2.4introduces threatmodeling,astructuredapproachtoanalyzingpotentialthreatsinKubernetes, and several security principles to create a hardening strategy when developing a Kubernetes system. 2.1 Containers and Docker Virtualizationistheabstractionofcomputerresourcesfromphysicalhardware[7]. Before the popularity of containers and Docker, virtualization technologies were alreadycommon. Forexample, virtualmachines, the ancestorofcontainers, arean approachthatprovidesanefficient,isolatedreplicaofacomputersystem’senvironment [8]. EachvirtualmachinehasitsownOSandsystemlibraries. Popularvirtualmachine providers are Microsoft Hyper-V, VirtualBox, and VMware [9, 10, 11]. The approach ofvirtualmachinesforvirtualizationprovidesdiverse andwell-establishedsoftware environments. However, a major issue with the traditional method is the performance of resource-intensive, high storage use, and slow start-up time. In contrast, using containers is the approach of visualizing only the OS, not the wholecomputer. Eventhoughthistechnologyhasbeeninvented,itbecamepopular andanindustrialstandardonlywithDocker, aplatformthatsupportsdevelopersto createandmanagecontainerseffectivelyanddeliverthesoftwarefaster. Thecontainer isastandardsoftwareunitthatpackagescodealongwithallitsdependencies,ensuring the application runs quickly and reliably across different computing environments [12]. The containers share the OS kernel, binaries, and libraries. There are many improvementscomparedtoVM,suchasalightweightprocess,nativeperformance, and a short startup time.

MinSu et al. demonstrate that Docker container outperforms virtualmachinesinresourceutilization,memoryusageanddiskI/O[7]. Thepackaged codeanddependenciescanworkinanyenvironment,includinglocal,development, staging, and production. In Figure 1, Docker’s architecture consists of •Client: The Docker client allows users to interact with the Docker daemon through its API. •Docker host: Docker host manages objects such as images and containers using the Docker daemon. Docker daemon listens to API requests from the client andinteractswiththeregistrytogetimages. Dockerdaemondoesmostofthe heavy tasks, such as pulling images from the registry, building, and running containers. •Registry: This is remote storage for images. To start containers, the Docker daemon pulls images from the registry in case images are not available in local

Figure 2: Kubernetes architecture 2.2.1 Kubernetes Architecture AKubernetesclustercomprisesa controlplane andmultiple workernodes. Thecontrol plane interacts with and manages all the worker nodes. In the control plane, there are differentcomponents: controlmanager,KubernetesAPIserver akakube-apiserver, scheduler, key-value database etcd. First, kube-apiserver’s main function is to expose the Kubernetes API for the cluster. It can be scaled horizontally using many instances to balance the workload [3]. It handles and validates all requests from worker nodes, developers, and cluster administrators [16]. Second, etcd is a consistent and highly available key-value store for the whole cluster [3]. It persistently stores internal state, metadata, secrets, and configuration data [16]. kube-apiserver is the only component that communicates and updates states in etcd. In addition, the kube-scheduler has the role of allocating a node to a Pod. TheworkernodeiswheretheapplicationsdefinedineachPodrun. Inaddition, two other Kubernetes components in the worker node are the kubeletandkube-proxy. KubeletmanagesPods,communicateswiththecontrolplane,managesthePodlife cycle,monitorsthenode’shealth,andmanagesthevolumes.

Additionally,kube-proxy manages services, balances message loading, and handles service discovery Kubernetes uses a declarative approach to manage all resources inside the cluster. The developer teams do not need to care about creating, deploying, or managing workload and resources. Everything works under the abstraction and configuration of Kubernetesresources. TounderstandhowKubernetesoperatesapplicationsandthe whole system, the next subsections discuss fundamental resources used in Kubernetes and related security challenges.

Figure 3: Example of Deployment architecture, with a workload replicated into three Pods. [3] 2.2.2 Pod Architecture InaKubernetessystem,aPodisabasicdeployableunit. Allapplicationsruninsidethe Pod. However, naked Pod usage is not recommended because it can’t be rescheduled in caseofnode failure. Aworkloadobjectis createdto abstractandmanage aPod, and it is easier to manage a Pod using a workload. Workload consists of Deployment, StatefulSet,DaemonSet,JobandCronJob. Deploymentisthemostcommonchoice among the workloads to run stateless applications. A Deployment’s architecture is described Figure 3 with a lower layer of ReplicaSet that keeps a desired number of Pods. For more advanced requirements using persistent data, StatefulSets support the managementofstatefulapplications. JobsandCronJobsareusedtohandleshort-lived tasks. 2.2.3 Service Discovery AsingleclustercanhavealargenumberofPodsrunning. AlthoughthosePodscanbe deployed in different machines (nodes), Kubernetes’s goal is to hide this to the Pods, which don’t need to understand the underlying processes. One of the challenges of servicediscoveryisthesize. Bigcompanies’clustersmayhavethousandsofnodes runningin a cluster. For example, OpenAIruns 7500nodes fora systemof different AImodelssuchasGPT,CLIP,DALL-E[17]. Anotherchallengeistheflexibilityof theworkloads. While eachPodhas itsown IPaddress,it isnotpractical touseit for routingdata. First,PodsareephemeralsinceasinglePodcanbefailed,destroyed,and recreated anytime.

Section: Hardening Application Deployment in Kubernetes > Page 16. Thus, the IP address of Pods could be different from the previous Pod version. In addition, when horizontally scaled, new Pods are created with new IPaddresses,andthereisnowaytokeeptrackofthesePods. Second,usingaPod’s address does not support loadbalancing, andrequests willnotbe distributedevenly to all healthy Pods. Kuberneteshasamechanismfornetworkabstractioncalled Services. TheServices,

asopposedtoPods,havestableIPaddressesandaloadbalancingfunctiontoPod’s loose coupling with Pod resources. Requests sent to an application would be routed to the service resource and then distributed to the Pod with the container running the application. Pods targeted by a Service are definedusing selectors andlabels. While selectors are defined in Services, labels are attached to Pods. They are both key-value pairs used by Services to identify the correct Pods. As a result, the requests are routed to the Services as a fixed endpoint, which hides the dynamic nature of Pods and their constant changes. 2.2.4 Configuration Data InKubernetes, ConfigMap andSecretaretwoobjectsusedfordataconfiguration. They are basically key-value data that has a decoupled life cycle from the Deployment. The maindifferenceisthatConfigMapsstoreplaintextdatasuchasenvironmentvariables, configurationfilesorconnectionURLs,whileSecretsstoredatainbase64-encoded form. Data in Secret is confidential, such as passwords, API keys, SSH keys, and OAuth. Secretdataisnotencryptedbydefaultintheetcddatabase. Anyonewithpermission toaccesstheAPIcanfetchandmodifythesecretcontent. Alldataisstoredinplain text, butKubernetesoffers some securityoptions to enhance protection forsecrets.

TomakedatainaSecretobjectsecure,therequiredstepsfromthemainKubernetes document are: •Enable encryption at rest [3] •Configure RBAC rules with least-privilege access [3] •Limit access to Secrets to specific containers only [3] 2.2.5 Customer Resource and Operators Inadditiontothemaincomponentssuchasworkloads,services, anddata,Kubernetes provides extensions for software running in the cluster. Custom resource is a powerful extensionbesidesthedefaultKubernetesresource[3]. Customerresourcesarecreated usingCustomResourceDefinition (CRD)or AggregatedAPI. CRDallowsdefiningnew types of Kubernetes resources without requiring a new API server, while Aggregated APIsenableservingresourcesfrommultiple,independentAPIservers. Usingboth the Custom Resource Definition and the bound controller is the most familiar method touseanOperator. Thisprovidestheclusterecosystemwithmoreabilities,suchas add-ons, community extensions [3]. Operators or the operator pattern are software extensions that utilize the customer resources for extended behaviors [3]. It creates a higher-level abstraction with functionality. Operators support automation in many tasks including deploying, restoring application state, or choosing a leader in a distributed environment. This is the complicated pattern with hard usage in the Kubernetes ecosystem, but really powerful. Common tools developed in the Kubernetes ecosystem are operator-based, such as Strimzi Kafka and Trivy Operator [18, 19].

2.3 DevOps DevOpsistheintegrationofculturalphilosophies,practices,andtoolsthatenhancean organization’s power to deliver products and services quickly. This approach enables organizationstogrowandimprovetheirproductsatanimpressiveratecomparedto thoseusingtraditionalsoftwaredevelopmentandinfrastructuremanagementprocesses. [20]. Development and operations teams collaborate throughout the entire application lifecycle,fromdevelopmentandtestingtodeploymentandoperations. Theydevelopa widerangeofskillsbeyondasinglefunction[20]. Thecommonissueofamicroservices systemthatleveragesaKubernetesscalingenvironmentistherequirementofrapid, safe, and reliable delivery of these complex microservices. DevOps practices such as continuousintegrationandcontinuousdeliverysolvethisissue. Itiswidelyadopted now and is a part of the modern software development process with microservices and Kubernetes as standard practices and technology choices. Different from the traditional and sequential Waterfall process, the DevOps life cycle is a system of parallel and continuous loop of constant flow. Constant feedbacks from operations areimmediatelyinstructedforthenextdevelopmentcycle. TheDevOpslifecycleis typically broken down into several stages [21]: •Plan: Define the requirements, create a road map, and plan the development process.

This stage involves gathering feedback, prioritizing tasks, and setting goals. •Code: Developers write code to implement features or fix bugs, adhering to coding standards and best practices. •Build: Compilethecode,packageitintoartifacts,andprepareitfortestingor deployment. •Test: Validatethecodeandartifactstoensuretheymeetqualitystandardsand function as expected. •Release: Package the tested code into a deployable artifact and prepare it for deployment to production or staging environments. •Deploy: Deploytheapplicationorupdatestoproductionorstagingenvironments, often using automated processes. •Operate: Manageandmaintaintheapplicationinproduction,ensuringitruns smoothly and efficiently •Monitor: Continuously observe the application and infrastructure to ensure performance, availability, and security. 2.4 Security Approaches By default, the dynamic environment of Kubernetes exposes many security risks, andsecuringthisischallenging. Securityframeworksandguidelinesarecommonly

used in industry to cover vulnerabilities for the best security practices. It supports understanding the best practices for attack and defense. 2.4.1 Threat Modeling InthecontextofKubernetessecurity,threatmodelingisaneffectivewaytodetermine possible threats and examine the effect of those threats. Furthermore, this helps to mitigate and prevent common threats. NSA describes three primary categories of threats in Kubernetes clusters and their sources of compromise, including supply chain,malicious threat actor, andinsider threat [5]. In the scope of this thesis, the primary threats are the supply chain and the malicious threat actor. •Supply chain: Supply chain cyberattack is carried out to gain unauthorized accesstothecluster. Thisisconductedbyvariedattackvectorsandischallenging to defend against. This can impact multiple levels of the Kubernetes cluster, including: –Container/application level: Not all containers are secure. Attackers can perform initial access tactics that exploit malicious containers and containers with malicious dependencies. –Containerruntime: This is the software that manages system resources, separates and executes containers on the node.

The vulnerabilities in containers lead to inadequate isolation between containers. –Infrastructure: The cluster is not secured if there is a foothold in the underlyingsystemhostingthatcluster. Thevulnerabilitiesmaycomefrom the software and hardware dependencies. •Malicious threat actor: Malicious threat actor in the context related to Kubernetes is a person or organization that causes harm to the cluster and exploits the inside components. The actors can target cluster-exposed APIs, including: –Controlplane: ControlplanecomponentsalwaysexposeAPIsforcommunication. There are many attack surfaces that can be exploited from the unprotected APIs. –Workernodes: Workernodesalsohavemanycomponentsusedforcommunication, such as kubelet and kubeproxy. Cyber actors can take advantage of these components to access the running nodes. –Containerized applications: Applications deployed and running in the cluster can be accessed from outside the cluster. An attacker can later escalate privileges in the Kubernetes environment. 2.4.2 Security Principles Securityprinciplesarethehighest-levelabstractionconceptsandphilosophiesused for decision-making in hardening systems. According to Liz et al., three principles

canbe usedtoimplementa securityframeworkforKubernetes,including defensein depth, the principle of least privilege, and limiting the attack surface [22]. They work together to reduce risk and ensure strong protection. Defense in depth protects layers, and the principle of least privilege limits the access for components in each layer, and finally, the limiting the attack surface principle narrows down the public entry points. Defense in Depth Defense in Depth is a security system that integrates individuals, technology, and capabilities to establish various protections across separate layers of the system [23]. AKubernetesclusterhasmanyimplementationlayersbuiltontopofeachother. A threatactorhasvariedchoicesforattackingacluster. Theycanattackthecontainerized application or the workload running in that container at a higher level. In addition, the control plane and worker nodes are also ideal targets for exploitation. A single vulnerabilitycanbeexploitedeffectivelywithoutasoliddefense. Duetothis,there must be multiple layers of defense for a cluster.

Principle of Least Privilege To limit the damage caused by security, the principle of least privilege demands the most restrictive set of privileges possible to perform a task [24]. Kubernetes components should be granted with the least permissions to function and operate [25]. Therefore, an actor with access to over-granted permissions resources can perform unauthorized actions using these permissions. Combined with the defense in depth principle, there must be several configurationsto limit permissions andreduce privileges in different layers. Limiting the Attack Surface Pratyusaetal. definedattacksurfaceasthesetofwaysinwhichanadversarycanenter the system and potentially cause damage [26]. In Kubernetes, components expose APIs for communication, especially the kube API and the kubelet. Even though these componentsarenotrevealedtotheexternalside,limitingtheattacksurfaceonthem restrains the chance of lateral movement. Moreover, endpoints for sending requests to Pods have also been published. On top of that, more and more access points would be opened as the cluster size grows. Consequently, this reveals more attack vectors into a cluster. Shift Left Security ShiftleftsecuritywasaphilosophyproposedbyLarrySmithin2021. Thisconcept focusesonsecurityprocessesthatareconductedintheearlystagesofdevelopment rather than waiting until the end [27]. Shift left security can be applied in the context ofKubernetesandDevOpsusingbestpracticesineachstagelistedin2.3. Conducting the security processes in the early stage may require effort, but it is necessary because theKubernetessystemismorecomplexthanthetraditionalone. Morespecifically,

thisemphasizestheeffectivenessofdoingsecurityasearlyinthelifecycleaspossible. Forexample,itiseasiertodetectavulnerabilitybystaticallyanalyzingacontainer before its deployment than trying to detect something erroneous at runtime. This approach allows for catching the security problems in the early stages and reduces the management of these issues in later stages [27]. 2.4.3 Security Frameworks Currently, there are several available frameworks for Kubernetes security. The frameworksincludeasetofguidelinesandbestpracticestomanagerisksystematically. Common standard frameworks are the 4C’s model, NIST SP 800-190, and the Kubernetes Hardening Guide from the National Security Agency [3, 28, 5]. The 4C’s model stand for Cloud,Clusters,Containers, andCode. This framework aims to build a security process for these layers. For the Cloud layer, it focuses on infrastructure in Kubernetes, such as the control plane, nodes, etcd, and the API. Moreover, the security of the cloud provider is also a concern. The Cluster layer works on cluster components and the applications running. This layer has a list of areas including authentication, RBAC, network policies, secrets management, and Pod security standards.

The Container layer is generally about container and OSvulnerabilities, containerruntimeisolation, imagesigning andenforcement,and disallowing unprivileged users. The final layer is the Code layer. However, this is out of scope for Kubernetes [3]. TheNISTSP800-190documentprimarilyworksforapplicationcontainertechnologies. Itraisesalistofmajorrisksforthecorecomponentsandsuggestscountermeasures fortherisks. Unfortunately,thisframeworkhasalimitedscopethatonlyworksforthe containers running inside the Kubernetes cluster [28]. TheKubernetesHardeningGuidedocumentisasecuritydocumentforadministratorsanddevelopersofUnitedStatesnationalsecuritysystems. Itcoverssecurity issues associated with improper configuration [5]. All the frameworks and guides abovearevaluableforuseinrealcloudsystems. Theyhelptoreducechallengesfor the complex nature of Kubernetes and document the best practices at the moment. The Kubernetes team also provides a listofsecurity practices thatfocus on control plane protection, secrets, workload protection, admission control, and auditing [3]. 2.4.4 Threat Matrix for Kubernetes MITRE ATT&CK is a global knowledge base of adversary strategies derived from applicationobservations. The ATT&CK isemployedasabasisfordesigningthreat modelsandmethodologiesfordiverseorganizations. [29]. Therearecurrently211 techniqueswith468sub-techniquesintheEnterprise ATT&CK matrixatthetimethis thesis is conducted, and MITRE continuously adds more. The techniques describe how adversaries perform attack actions. TheATT&CK matrixisthefoundationfortheKubernetesthreatmatrix, which can be seen in Figure 4. The Kubernetes threat matrix is a simplified version that was createdbytheAzureSecurityCenter. Thismatrixconsistsofattacktechniquesthat

Figure 4: Threat matrix for Kubernetes [30] are relevant to orchestration security in Kubernetes [30]. This matrix is usable for identifying attack vectors and prioritizing defenses for any Kubernetes system. The main techniques include: •Initial Access: Initial access techniques are attack methods to gain access to the resources. Attackers can gain this access through cloud credentials, compromised images, vulnerable applications, and exposed interfaces. •Execution: Executiontechniquesaremethodsusedtorunmaliciouscodeinside a cluster. Attackers canuse malicious commands, runtheir owncontainer, and run code inside the deployed application. •Persistence: Persistencetechniquesareusedtokeeptheattackerswithaccess to the cluster. Attackers may do it using backdoor containers, hostPath volume mounts, a directory, and Kubernetes CronJobs to run malicious code. •Privilege escalation: These techniques are used by attackers to get higher privileges in the environment than those they currently have [30]. This can bedonebyaccessingaprivilegedcontainerorbyhavinghighpermissionsto resources such as hostPath mounts and cluster-admin bindings. •Defenseevasion: Defenseevasionisatechniqueusedtohidetheactivitiesof attackers. Evidence such as container logs and Kubernetes events is cleared. AttackerscanalsocreatesimilarlynamedbackdoorPodsthatlookidenticalto Kubernetes’ main components. •Credentialaccess: Attackersusecredentialaccesstechniquestostealcredentials, includingKubernetesSecrets,serviceaccounts,andcredentialsinconfiguration files.

•Discovery: Attackers use discovery techniques to explore the environment to which they gained access. •Lateralmovement: Attackersuselateralmovementtomovethroughthevictim’s environment. •Impact: Impact techniques lead to consequences such as data destruction, resource hijacking, and denial of service. This can be performed by destroying resources,configuration,andstorage,andmanipulatingcompromisedresources. 2.4.5 Kubernetes CVEs TheCommon Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVEs) is a list of documented vulnerabilities and exposures commonly found in typical applications [16]. A CVE’s record describes a vulnerability using a description, root cause, severity of the issue, and references to the details of the issue published by the researchers. Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) is used as scores assigned to a single CVE record[16]. TheCVEpublicdatabasecouldbeeffectivelyusedtoaddresssecurity risks and reduce the attack surface of a Kubernetes cluster.

3 Literature Review ThischapterprovidesaliteraturereviewonKubernetesclustersecuritybestpractices. Theliteraturereviewisstructuredtoanalyzethebestpracticesindiverselayersand components. Section 3.1 explores approaches for protecting a typical Kubernetes cluster. Section 3.2 delves deeper into safeguarding the workload and pod, which represent the core of the application. In the lower layer of workload, section 3.3 covers techniques to make Docker images and containerized applications safe for usage. Section 3.4 displays how tomonitor and what to monitor for eachcomponent and each layer. Section 3.5 focuses on compliance checks and methods to protect components that operate the system, such as control plane components and worker node components. Finally, section 3.6 represents the current gap of these practices and what is missing for a team to apply in software development. 3.1 Cluster Security At the highest cluster level, certain practices can influence the entire cluster. This sectionoutlines variouspracticesthatallcontributeto creatingasecurecluster. These practices include secret management, authentication, authorization, and managing namespace resource quotas. Typically, these tasks fall under the responsibilities of the cluster administrator. 3.1.1 Namespace Boundaries Kubernetesnamespacesaremeanttoseparateresourcesformultipleteamswithina cluster.

Thereare3defaultnamespaceincluding kube-system,kube-public,default. Whilekube-systemandkube-publicnamespacescannotbedeleted,theyshouldnotbe utilizedtoorganizeresources. Customnamespacesprovideaninitiallayerofisolation betweenresources,especiallyusefulinmulti-tenantenvironments. Customnamespaces canfacilitateresourceisolation,effectivelyestablishingavirtualsegmentwithspecific rules, resource usage, and networktraffic controls. Additionally, developers can setup RBAC, resource limits, and more advanced configurations to enhance security [5]. However, Kubernetes Operators need privileges and may connect to resources across differentnamespaces[31]. Andongetal. identifiedvulnerabilitiesincross-namespace referenceswithinKubernetesOperatorduetodissimilaritybetweenthedeclaredscope of a resource and the actual scope of its process logic [31]. The same research shows that 317 out of 2268 Operators on GitHub are vulnerable to cross-namespace attacks. Consequently, lateral movement is not prevented by default between namespaces without advanced configuration. 3.1.2 Secrets Management CoderunningintheKubernetesclustergenerallyretrievessecretdata. Thesesecret data, which are stored using secret objects by Kubernetes, can be API keys, OAuth tokens, and SSH keys [5]. Automatically, the resource is an etcd key-value database,

anditisnotencryptedwithoutconfiguration[3]. Thisisacommontargetintheattack vector. This subsection discusses methods to work with secret and secure sensitive information. Secret Encryption And Secret Storage Encryptionistheprimarychoicetoprotectsecretvalues. Arshetal. studyexplains thatusingcryptographyandencryptionisamainwaytosecuredataonthecloud[32]. InKubernetes,secretsareinsecurelystoredintheetcddatabaseinbase64encoding by default. It can be encrypted at rest and in transit [3]. Besides the etcd database, secret values can be stored in third-party solutions such as HashiCorp Vault. This third-party tool supports secret storage with strong encryption for data at rest, in use, and in transit, and offers many advanced features such as auto-rotating secrets and dynamic secrets. Secret Rotation And Revocation In secret management, secret rotation is the process of regularly updating the secret. Secret rotation is an important practice to protect sensitive data. A secret can be safe from human guessing, but not safe from machine attacks. Shortening the period of a secretmakesbruteforceattacksharderandmorecomplex. Eventhoughthesecretsare compromised, threat actors only have a limited time to perform unauthorized actions using the leaked key.

This is not a built-in function of the Kubernetes system, but alternative third-party solutions, such as HashiCorp Vault, offer this function [22]. Secretrevocationisequallyvital. Secretrevocationistheactionofinvalidatingthe secret. This helps to ensure that the old key or compromised secret can’t be exploited. For example, after leaving the organization, an employee can’t use the existing secret anymore. Apart from the main methods for secrets protection using key and encryption, Shazibul et al. also recommend spreading sensitive workload to prevent escalation on a compromised node [33]. Kavindu et al. simplified secrets management with K8s ProSentinel,aGUI-basedOperatorthatsupportscreating,modifying,andauditing secrets[34]. However,therelatedacademicliteratureisstillfarfromacomprehensive solution for secrets management. 3.1.3 Authentication and Authorization Bothauthentication andauthorization areveryimportantforanapplication. While authentication is used to verify the identity of the actor, authorization is later used to verify the permission associated with the request [16]. In Kubernetes, the core component that exposes the API and verifies all requests is the kube-apiserver [16]. Authentication Authentication is the initial step in Kubernetes security, verifying actors, services or applications that are trying to access the API. There are many mechanisms for

authentication, including static tokens, basic authentication with a password, service account tokens, bootstrap tokens, webhook tokens, authentication proxy tokens, client certificates, and user impersonation [16]. All methods can be enabled simultaneously. Authorization Afterauthenticationistheauthorizationprocess. Theauthorizationmoduledetermines the permission of the identified user or service and returns the decision to allow or deny. There are many authorization modes, such as Node authorization, ABAC mode, RBAC, AlwaysDeny, AlwaysAllow, and webhooks [22]. Each option has its own pros andcons, butRBACis themostwidelyusedandrecommendeddespiteits complexity [25,35]. AlwaysDenymodeblocksalltherequests,andAlwaysAllowmodeallowsall requests. Theyshouldbeusedonlyfortestinganddevelopment, notforthereleased production. 3.1.4 Role-based Access Control Role-based access control(RBAC) is an authorization method that works based on the roles of users [25]. Roles with a set of permissions are created and assigned to users. The scopes of using RBAC are cluster-wide and namespace-wide [22]. The four core objects used for RBAC are Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, and ClusterRoleBindings.

There are use cases to use these objects: •Use Roles and RoleBindings to create roles and assign them to users in the namespace scope. •Reuse role with ClusterRoles in different namespaces and assign to users using RoleBindings. •UseClusterRolestocreatecluster-widepermissionsandassigntousersusing ClusterRoleBindings [22]. 3.1.5 Network Policies In the Kubernetes environment, the network model is flat, and all traffic is allowed in theclusternetwork. Thismeansthateverypodcancommunicatewithanyotherpodin the cluster freely, even in different namespaces. This approach simplifies the network design in the cluster [25]. Network policies have been introduced to control the traffic betweenpods. Networkpoliciesareresourcesthatdefinehowtrafficisroutedinthe network. They control what pods can send or receive requests. Ingress and egress rules are the rules used to control the traffic. Ingress defines rules for incoming traffic, and egress defines rules for outgoing traffic. Consequently, thepodingresstrafficofthedestinationmustmatchtheegresstrafficofthesource. Thebestpracticesrecommendapplyingthenetworkpoliciestoallpods. Itisriskyfor teamstodesignnetworkpoliciesbutaccidentallyforgetdetailsandleaveitunsecured. Agoodpracticehereisto defaultdenypolicy [25]. Consequently, alltrafficisblocked until new policies are created for the pod.

NetworkpolicyconstructionissupportednativelybyusingKubernetesnetwork policies. They can also be created using a CNI plugin such as Cilium and Calico. Daniele et al. developed automatic network generation [36]. Now, there are more unexplored tools invented to support generating policies, such as Helm-ET and NeuVector[37,38]. However,designingnetworkpoliciesisstillchallengingforteams and requires significant effort. After policy generation, policy verification becomes a concern. A tool suchas Kano, which utilizes formalverification models, is available for this purpose [39]. Later, Shunbin et al. proposed a faster method for network policy verification that uses bitwise compression. [40]. 3.1.6 Namespace Resource Quotas Thenamespaceresourcequota isamechanismusedtocontrolresourcelimitsavailable toallobjectsinnamespacescope[16]. ItisrecommendedbyHamedetal. thatdifferent teams work on the same cluster [41]. The resource quota can manage maximum resourcerequestsandlimitsforCPUandmemory,thesumofstoragerequests,and finally, the number of different objects in a namespace.

It uses the ResourceQuota object, which is illustrated in an example 1 below: apiVersion: v1 kind: ResourceQuota name: compute-resources-demo hard: requests.cpu: "1" requests.memory: "1Gi" limits.cpu: "2" limits.memory: "2Gi" requests.nvidia.com/gpu: 4 Listing 1: ResourceQuota configuration for Kubernetes [16] The example of resource quota 1 apply theses configurations into a namespace: •request.cpu: Limits the total sum of CPU requests across all pods in the namespace to 1 CPU core [16]. •request.memory: Limits thetotal sum ofmemory requests across allpods to 1 gigabyte [16]. •limit.cpu: LimitsthetotalsumofCPUlimitsacrossallpodsto2CPUcores. Limits define the maximum CPU a pod can use. [16]. •limit.memory: Limits the total sum of memory limits across all pods to 2 gigabyte. [16]. •requests.nvidia.com/gpu: Limits the total number of NVIDIA GPUs requested bypodsinthe namespaceto4. ThisisspecifictoenvironmentswithNVIDIA GPU support configured. [16].

3.1.7 Dynamic Admission Control DynamicAdmissionControlisacomponentinKubernetesthatinterceptsrequests to the API server. It has an advanced function to allow customized configurations without impact on the kube-apiserver [3]. Dynamic admission control, implemented via admission webhooks, significantly strengthens Kubernetes security by providing a flexible andproactive mechanismfor enforcingcustom securitypoliciesat thepoint ofresourcecreationormodification. Itcansafeguardclusterresourcesbothduring runtimeandatdeployment. Intheruntimephase,theadmissioncontrollerprevents unauthorized access and configuration changes. Unlike built-in controllers, it can becustomizedtoaddressspecificsecuritythreats[42]. Duringpoddeployment,the dynamic admission controller can reject any deployments that violate established policies. It can be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline, aligning with modern software development processes and integrating with image scanning tools to prevent the deployment of vulnerable images [43]. 3.2 Workload and Pod Level Security Workloads or pods are the most fine-grained means to run the main business logic of the application. Therefore, they should be taken care of. This subsection discusses security issues related to configuration to protect a single workload running in the cluster.

3.2.1 Pod Security Standards and Pod Security Admission Securing configurations in Kubernetes workload presents significant challenges. An improperly configured pod may be vulnerable to exploitation, which could potentially compromisetheintegrityoftheentirecluster. PodSecurityStandards(PSSs)makethe security configuration painless and efficient. PSSs are Kubernetes policies for pods to ensure that all pods in the cluster adhere to consistent security baselines, minimizing vulnerabilitieswhilemaintainingoperationalflexibilitywithdifferentlevels. ThePSA enforces these policies. Three levels of Pod Security Standards are: •Privileged: Openpolicyprovidingthewidestlevelofpermissions. Itpermits some privilege escalations. [3]. •Baseline: Policythatminimallyrestrictsandcontrolssomeprivilegeescalations. It permits the default configuration for Pods with minimal specifications. [3]. •Restricted: Themostrestrictedone,usinghardeningbestpracticesforPods[3]. Pod Security Admission (PSA) is a built-in admission controller embedded in the kube-apiserver that enforces the PSS. If a request violates the policies defined in the PSS, PSA will react in 3 different modes: •enforce: Policy violations result in the rejection of the pod. [3].

•audit: Policy violations lead to an audit annotation being added to the event recorded in the audit log, while they are still permitted. [3]. •warn: Policy violations result in a warning for the user. However, they are generally permitted. [3]. PSS and PSA work together as a two-part system. PSS provides the policy framework, while the PSA module operates it through namespace labels and real-time validationofpodcreationandupdateperiods. ThisduoisKubernetes’primarypod securitymechanism,ensuringclustersaresecure,flexible,andeasytomanage. An admin labels a namespace with a PSS level. When a pod is created, PSA reads the namespace label, fetches the corresponding PSS policy, and validates the pod’s spec. Depending on the configured policies and PSA mode set, PSA may reject, log data, or trigger an alert. 3.2.2 Deploy Honeypot Workload and Intrusion Detection Systems Canary deployment is a strategy to deploy multiple versions of an application and routeasmallamountoftrafficfromasmallusersubsettothenewpodversionsbefore fully rolling out. This technique can be applied in the Kubernetes environment. Fake workloads and services are deployed in the cluster.

These resources act as a honeypot and are intentionally exposed to vulnerabilities in the cluster to monitor and notify the team of intrusions and attackers. Attackers can not differentiate between real and fake ones. Anomalous patterns will be monitored and detected by IDS [25]. After the attack actions are detected, the attack is traced back to the initial vector and alerted to the team [25]. The setup for this approach is presented in Figure 5. When an attacker sendsmalicioustraffictotheKubernetescluster,theyarelikelytotargetthevulnerable pod,whichisactuallythehoneypot. Thisdivertstheattackerawayfromtheprivileged pod, which contains the actual sensitive data or critical workloads. OtherstudiesshowthathoneypotisaneffectiveweaponinKubernetessecurity. For example, Noah et al. built a honey pod system using Docker, Kubernetes, and workflow tools such as Apache Airflow, Spinnaker, and Argo for 3 months to uncover the risk associated with a Kubernetes system [44]. They received 191 attacks and categorized4maintypesofattackontheircluster,includingexploitingtheexposed service, establishing persistence on the hijacked system, cryptocurrency mining, checking for updates, or continuing to spread.

In detail, the cluster was attacked by 3 big groups and others by diverse methods. The activities on containers are installing a rootkit,containerescape,establishingpersistence,disablinghostdefenses,installing crypto-miners and launching an unprivileged container. In the Kubernetes layers, they initially exploited the API server, kubelet, and the exposed endpoints. A similar research from Chakshu et al. is conducted on GKE honey pot system in 2 weeks to collect attack data from 200 IP adresses [45]. They recorded 11,500 brute force attempts, and the successful ones have similar behavior to mining cryptocurrency usingthecluster’sCPUs. Alltheseresearchstudiesrevealbeneficialinformationabout thecommonattackvectorsofKubernetesinreal-worldscenariosandtheattackers’ behavior.

Figure 5: Kubernetes honeypot [25] 3.2.3 Static Analysis of YAML file and configuration YAMLfilesarecreatedduringthedevelopmentprocess. Thesefilescanbecreatedby developers without much regard for security practices, or they simply make it wrong. AYAMLfileconfigurationcanbeextremelycomplicatedwithmanypropertiesand indentation without a clear guide and standard, and they do not know how to securely generate YAML files for Kubernetes objectssuch as workloads and services. Wrong configurationsinYAMLfilesagainexposedtheattacksurfaceandledtounpredictable consequences. Akondetal. identifymaincategoriesofmisconfigurationcategories with correlated Kubernetes objects, collected in Table 2 from [46]: Kubernetes Object Misconfiguration Load Balancing for Meshes Insecure HTTP Secret Hard-coded secret Pod AbsentsecurityContext, AbsentResourceLimit, Activation ofhostPID, Activation ofhostIPC, Activation ofhostNetwork, EscalatedPrivileges for Child Container Processes, Insecure HTTP Stateful Applications PrivilegedsecurityContext,ActivationofhostNetwork, Capability Misuse Process Execution Activation ofhostPID, Activation ofhostIPC, Activation of hostNetwork, Docker Socket Mounting Traffic Routing Insecure HTTP Table 2: Kubernetes misconfigurations by object type Aside from the listed categories, another study conducted on 287 open-source applications by Bufalino et al. identifies misconfigurations mainly related to the service labels and network configuration collision [47]. To mitigate this type of misconfiguration, it is recommended to use static scanning and analyze YAML files. Thestatictools,suchas kubesecorkubetestcandetectdemandingflagsrequiredfor security [22].

3.2.4 Resource Management for Pods According to Eunsook et al., CPU-intensive pods lead to degradation of cluster performance [48]. The same research shows a decrease in half of the CPU utilization. When a newpod is created, the kube-schedulermonitors the resources of the worker nodes and tries to assign the new pod to one of them. It also points to another root cause of high CPU usage, which is that network workload consumes and raises a researchquestionforfurtherresourcemanagementofKubernetes. Inanotherresearch, Tan et al. created the AlloX scheduler using Go andPython to optimize the dynamic fair allocation of CPU and GPU resources on a Kubernetes cluster for machine learning jobs [49]. Zhang et al. designed a scheduling algorithm, CloudSim, which is claimed to be better than the original scheduling algorithm and reduces cost [50]. For developers at the moment, resource requests and limits allow a simpler and default way of allocation and limitation of resources to Kubernetes objects.

To do it, it needs tocomputeresourcesrequestedforthepod,andthiscanbeconfiguredintheworkload YAML manifest or a specific pod object. Resource management at this level allocates andlimits resourcesin asingleKubernetes object. Configuration canbedetermined forCPUandmemory[16]. Forinstance,thelisting12isthebodyofamanifestfile thatindicatesrequestresourcelimitationwithhalfcoresand300megabytes. However, accordingto[41],itisdangeroustostartthepodwithoutanaccurateresourcelimit specification. If the total resource limits exceed the total capacity of a node, and each node attempts to use resources up to its specified limit, the Linux OS kernel will ignoretheselimits. Itwillinsteadallocateonlytherequestedresourcesortheresource allocation will be adjusted according to the ranges specified by Kubernetes. To crack this issue, Hamed et al. proposed a model to equitably calculate and allocate resource limits among the pods [41]. 3.3 Container Security Developers typically work with containers and images. Their responsibilities include buildingcontainersforapplications,andsecurityisacriticalaspectthatneedsattention. However,conducting thesecuritytasks ischallenging. A researchbyDongminet al. exploresmisconfigurationsin2,680exposedDockerRegistryHTTP411APIsand844 Dockerdaemoncomponentsfrom14largecloudproviders. Thesameresearchalso discovered 36 misconfigured servers in the Fortune 500 and 21 in diverse government servers[51]. Thiscriticalhardeningprocessshouldbeginduringtheimagecreation process with the appropriate configurations to limit exposure, followed by scanning at differentstages. Thissectionwilldetailhowtocreatesecureimagesandcontainers for development teams. 3.3.1 Build Secure Image In the Kubernetes environment, applications are deployed using container images, which are typically stored in registries such as Docker Hub or private repositories. The image defines application code, dependencies, and runtime environment. It is

criticalthatthebuiltimagebesecuredtoremovetheattacksurface. Toaddressthis, several practices are taken when building a secure image for Kubernetes [25, 22]: •AppArmor is a Linux security module that supports configuring and managing container permissions on files and resources of container processes. Hui et al. created Kub-Sec, an automatic AppArmor policy generator for the whole distributed Kubernetes cluster. [52]. •It is recommended to use a minimal version of the trusted base image, such asanimmutableLinuxdistribution,Alpine,ordistrolessimagesfromtrusted sources, to reduce the attack surface. The base images should only comprise runtime dependencies for the application. [25] •In addition to minimizing image size, debug utilities such as shells, file system utilities can be removed [22]. This presents an attacker with the ability to escalate using common tools and reduces the attack surface. •Kubernetesdoesnothaveimageverification. Thiscanbesolvedbyusingimage signingandaKubernetesadmissioncontroller. Theadmissioncontrollercan enforce a policy requiring image signatures and verify the image’s validity. •Afterdeployment,manycontainersrunasrootusers. Anattackercanexploit and compromise the whole system.

Creating non-privileged users can be run in the image Dockerfile configuration [25]. •Even images can be stored in both private and public registries, it should be stored in a private registry to control read and write permissions and grant read-only accounts for pulling images [22] 3.3.2 Image Scanning Imagescanningshouldbedoneatmultiplestagesoftheapplication,includingthebuild stage, the deploy stage, and the runtime stage. The scanner is usually integrated with theCI/CDpipeline. ThescannedinformationincludesOSpackages,dependencies, file systems, passwords, certificates, and binaries. The scanner tries to collect data from the file system and matchit with a vulnerabilitydata source such as CVE [25]. Even if an image passes the static scanning and is deployed in the runtime stage, new critical vulnerabilities can be found and updated in the database [16]. There are many scanner tools available, including open-source and commercial ones, but choosing oneshouldbeconsideredbasedonprivacyconcerns. Sensitivedatamaybeleaked from the image, then stored and misused. Image Scanning in development phase Automationforimagesscanningtodetectvulnerabilitiesintheearlystageisabest practice[22]. Inthedevelopmentstage,theimageisfirstcreatedandoptionallypushed into the registry. The scanner later analyses image layers with existing vulnerabilities

in the database. The build pipeline should stop and reject an image that fails the scanning process [22]. Image Scanning in deployment phase Evenifimagescanningisdoneduringdevelopment,itcanbeactivatedinnumerous stages, especially with the adoption of DevOps. New vulnerabilities can be found during deployment scanning, even if they pass the first scanning in development. It is the best practice to scan images in multiple stages of software development. TheValidatingAdmissionWebhook is an admission controller designed to support validating requests to the kube-apiserver. It checks images belonging to workloads thatarereadyfordeployment. Iftheimagesdonotmeetthedefinedsecurityscanning requirements, the workload will not be accepted. Sysdighasdevelopedanopen-sourceprojectcalled AnchoreEngine tofacilitatethis process. Here’showitworksinconjunctionwiththeValidatingAdmissionWebhook [16]: •A request to create a workload is transmitted to the kube-apiserver and then forwardedtoValidatingAdmissionWebhook. Itwilllaterbeprocessedbythe Sysdig scanning tool. •The scanning tool evaluates the image and returns the results to the kubeapiserver. •Basedonthescanningresults,thekube-apiservermakesthefinaldecisionto either approve or reject the workload creation request. •In summary, the decision to reject or approve the workload lies with the kube-apiserver, guided by the results from the scanning tool.

Image Scanning at the runtime stage Afterbeingdeployedtothecluster,imagesarefrequentlyre-scannedbythescannerto checkfornewvulnerabilitiesinthedatabase. Itisrecommendedtotriggerthescanning process when new data is updated to NVD [16, 25]. In case a new vulnerability is detected, the failed scan can alert the operator to take action quickly. 3.3.3 Container Threat Analysis Beyondtraditionalimage scanning,themore advancedandnewsecuritypractice for theimagesiscontainerthreatanalysis. Thisisasandbox-basedsolutionthatutilizes machine learning and smart algorithms to detect vulnerabilities in running containers. It tracks the network traffic, processes, system calls, memory, and other behaviors. Furthermore,filesystemsarecheckedtodetectmalwareandthreats[25]. Thismethod isgaining popularitywithsolutionsdeliveredin themarket,suchas AquaSecurity’s Dynamic Threat Analysis, Google’s Container Threat Detection, and Sysdig Falco [53, 54, 55].

3.3.4 LimitRanger LimitRanger is a built-in admission controller, and LimitRange is an object in Kubernetes. LimitRangeisresponsibleforsettingminimum,maximum,anddefault resource limits (like CPU and memory) for containers and pods within a namespace. LimitRanger then verifies and rejects any request that violates the constraints [25] in LimitRange. Violated request will receive a 403 status code response [16]. The example13configurationbelowshowsthesettingoftheminimumof0.05CPUcores, maximumof0.5CPUcores,andmemorylimitof512mebibytesandminimum50 mebibytes of memory for the namespace demo. 3.4 Monitoring, Alerting and Auditing Whena Kubernetesclusteris running,itis theperfecttarget foranattack. Therefore should be strategies to protect it during the running time. Most of the configurations mentioned before protect the cluster from threats while it runs. Moreover, it can be pushed further with protection during runtime. This section discusses the three mostimportantstrategies in theruntime phaseofthe Kubernetes cluster, including monitoring the system, alerting errors and audit logs. Monitoring MonitoringintheKubernetessysteminvolvesmeasurementsandmetricsusedtocollect real-timedataaboutclusterhealthandresourceutilization, toalert[25]. Datausedfor monitoring are pods logs, network logs, application flow logs, and metrics that should bemonitoredareconnectionpersecond,datapersecond,requestpersecond,CPU,and memoryusage. Thisenablesproactivedetectionofbottlenecks,faileddeployments, and scaling needs, ensuring optimal operations and quick troubleshooting.

In addition to security, monitoring helps detect any degradation in the quality of service, which is presented in [56]. NotethatthenatureofKubernetesisadistributeddeployment. Themonitorhas tobeconductedonagroupofnodes. CombinedwiththecomplexityofKubernetes architecturewithmanypods,network,andthismakesmonitoringaresource-intensive taskandrequiresnecessaryconfigurationtobalance thevisibilityandexpansiveness [25]. Monitoring can be implemented using a third-party tool such as Prometheus and Grafana. Kubernetes provides metrics that are used for collecting data about pod and cluster-wide scope by Prometheus [25]. Grafana supports advanced functions for visualizingdata in chartsand creating valuableobservability for teams. Machine learningisalsoreallypromisingnowasamonitoringmechanism. Josueconducted research on machine learning for anomaly detection in Kubernetes, yielding effective results for alerting outliers and pledging broader applications in the future [57]

3.5 Compliance Check Compliance validation provides systematic verification that the Kubernetes system adheres to organizational policies and industry security standards. This process involves continuous auditing of cluster configurations against established benchmarks to identify deviations from security baselines. For Kubernetes environments, the CIS KubernetesBenchmark releasedbytheCentreforInternetSecurityandthe Kubernetes Hardening Guide by the National Security Agency are 2 ongoing industry standards. Adaptingtothesedocumentsisalsoabestpracticethatneedstobedoneduringthe development process. For a project using Kubernetes as an orchestration system, there could be a compliance team that is responsible forensuring compliance checks are adopted by the team and the software [25]. Therefore, following the shift left security principle, using these compliance documents during the development stage is an excellent choice to remove security risks in the early stage. 3.5.1 CIS Security Benchmark The CIS Kubernetes Benchmark [60], developed by the Centre for Internet Security, delivers prescriptive configuration guidelines for hardening Kubernetes clusters. It givessecurityrecommendationsforthedirectconfigurationofKubernetesresource components in the worker nodes and control plane.

Structured around five critical assessment domains, it addresses: •Control plane elements with API server, scheduler, controller manager [60] •Etcd configuration [60] •Control plane configuration [60] •Worker node components (kubelet and kube-proxy) [60] •Kubernetes policy enforcement [60] Allofthesecomponentsareinherentlypartofthecluster,andanycompromisecan leadtotheentireclusterbeingattacked. ThechecklistprovidedbytheCISBenchmark offers recommendations for hardening the resource components in the Kubernetes worker nodes and control plane. By using this configuration, teams have strong security settings on the available components in the Kubernetes architecture. Each iteminthechecklistreturnsdetailsaboutthedefaultconfigurationvalue,rationale, impact, audit, and remediation [60]. 3.5.2 Kubernetes Hardening Guide IncontrasttotheconcentrationinKubernetescontrolplaneandworkernoderesources. TheKubernetesHardeningGuidedocument[5],developedbytheNSAandCISA, spreads the checklist across all practices of Kubernetes. It encompasses a broad range ofKubernetespractices,includingpodsecurity,networkconfiguration,authentication, auditing, and vulnerability management. This broad scope ensures that organizations

cansecureallaspectsoftheirclusters,fromworkloaddeploymenttoruntimeprotection, makingitavaluableindustrydocument. Theguideshouldbeadoptedlogicallyduring thedevelopmentprocessusingthestructuredchecklistofactionablerecommendations, designed to mitigate three primary sources of compromise including supply chain attacks, malicious threat actors, and insider threats 3.5.3 Related Work in Compliance Check Complianceincloudcomputingisacomplicatedissuethatrequiresextensiveresearch bydeveloperteamsandorganizations. Amitetal. haveconductedacomprehensive study to show compliance models of 13 categories related to risk including: •"Data encryption, key management" [61] •"Media protection" [61] •"Identification, authentication and authorization" [61] •"Virtualization and resource abstraction" [61] •"Portability and interoperability" [61] •"Application security" [61] •"Security risk assessment and management" [61] •"Privacy, electronic discovery and other legal issues" [61] •"Contingency planning" [61] •"Data center operations, maintenance" [61] •"Incident response" [61] •"Compliance, audit and accountability" [61] •"Awareness and training" [61] In the cloud industry, cloud organizations have their own compliance solution. First,GoogleCloudSecurityCommandCenterisacentralizedplatformtomanagerisk in Google [62]. It can monitor compliance, detect violations, and enforce compliance. Similarly,otherprovidershavetheirownsolutions,suchasAWSConfigandAzure Policy, with similar functionalities in compliance checks [63, 64]. Prisma Cloud providesasolutionofAPI-basedcompliancemanagementservice[65]. Virtualization organizationsRedHatOpenShiftandVMwarealsohavetheircomplianceenforcement solution, such as OpenShift Container Platform and VMware Aria [66, 67]. They workforcomplianceenforcementandcheckingincontainersandinfrastructure. All these listed solutions by the organizations provide support for runtime compliance checking and the configuration of custom rules [68].

In the academic field, Many studies propose effective solutions for the compliance enforcement process. Mozhdeh et al. introduced a machine learning model that uses network traffic data to detect compliance violations of privacy policies with more than 86% accuracy [69]. Mehdi et al. proposed a real-time detection and recommendationonHIPAAcompliancemodel[70]. Ghareebetal. excellentlyprovide anewcompliancemanagementsolutionforinfrastructureascode[68]. Althoughthere are many documents about compliance for cloud systems, the available document specificallyforKubernetesisstilllimitedinbothresearchandindustryatthemoment.

3.6 Research Gaps SecuritypracticeshavebeenimprovedineachversionbytheKubernetesteamandthe securityteam. Newvulnerabilitieshavebeenreportedandrecorded,andnewtools used to harden Kubernetes clusters have been invented in the last few years. However, these works have also revealed some challenges and gaps: •There is no default secure configuration. The main document page from the Kubernetes team also provides configuration tips with simple and minimal configurationtoreduceerroroccurrence[3]. Thisincreasestheattackvector for inexperienced developers. •There is no systematic approach for security when building and deploying Kubernetesapplications. ThereareguidelinessuchastheKubernetesHardening GuidebytheNationalSecurityAgency,theCISKubernetesBenchmark,and the main Kubernetes security document [5, 60, 3]. However, when applying for a software product, these guides create overhead and may slow down the developmentprocess. Thereisaneed forclearinstructionsonhowto securea cluster for developers and system administrators. •A complex architecture of Kubernetes makes it extremely hard to build a comprehensive supply chain security. All the mentioned guides cannot propose afriendlyend-to-endsupplychainsecurityapproachforadevelopmentprocess. Thereshouldbeamethodtobuildbothefficientandsecuresoftwaredevelopment. The above research gaps highlight a need for a different security approach for developers and system administrators. This thesis primarily addresses these gaps and createsanewformalapproachforbuildingasecureKubernetescluster. Thisapproach neededtobecapableofbeingusedbydevelopersinthedevelopmentprocessingeneral cases. There aredifferent cloudKubernetes servicessuch asGKE, EKS, AKS,IKS, ACK,andon-premisesenvironments. Thenewhigh-levelapproachshouldworkfor all cases. In addition, this approach needs to translate high-level recommendations to somethingthatusersanddeveloperscaneasilyapplytothesystemwithoutslowing down the release cycles and collaboration process of teams.

4 Methodology The primary goal for a new method is to provide a comprehensive approach for Kubernetessecurity. Thisframework’stargetistocoverthecurrentgapsinthesecurity oftheKubernetessystemandotherframeworks. Thissectionisstructuredtointroduce the ideas and instructions to apply, and how the proposed method bridges the current gaps in Kubernetes security. Section 4.1 discusses the overview of a new method. Section 4.2 explores the details of the main methods and displays their structure. Section 4.3 finally shows how it can be applied by developers in a real system. 4.1 Methodology Overview Some Kubernetes security frameworks have been introduced in Security Frameworks, includingthe4C’sModel,NISTSP800-190,andKubernetesHardeningGuidance. However,theseframeworksjustgivealistofbestpracticesandrecommendations,and it is still far away from a comprehensive approach with fine-grained instructions for a Kubernetessystem. Thissectionintroducesanewapproachforusingmatrix-based Kubernetes security. An initial version of this matrix security is shown in Table 3. Kubernetes clusters and Docker containers are both included in the scope of this new approach, which is a difference from the frameworks introduced earlier. The scope islimitedonthesetechnologiesbecausetheyarethestandardindustrialonesatthe moment. There are other alternatives used in the market for containerisation and orchestration,suchasDockerSwarmandOpenShift.

However,duetothedifference inthenatureoftechnology,thismethodisnotguaranteedtobeappliedinthosecases. Another difference with other approaches is that this matrix is not just a siloed guidancewithalistofpractices. Thematrixisinspiredmainlybyusingideasfrom the shift-left security anddefense-in-depth principles introduced in section Security Frameworks. Martinetal. recommendimplementingadefense-in-depthstrategyfor software applications [71]. Consequently, this approach is an effective wayto defend different layers of Kubernetes systems. Research by Zlatan et al. has the same idea to conductdefense indepthindifferent layersas amainstrategy tosecureKubernetes. They split the layers into components into control plane, node security, image and workload, access management, observability and auditing [72]. In contrast, this thesis work divides layers by architectural structure of a cluster from the container to the pod and cluster-wide level. It is crucial to apply the methodology across different layers cause Garsha claimed that Kubernetes has a layered architecture [35]. Each layerhasitsownsecurityrequirementsandsettings. Thislayeredarchitecturerequires defenseindepth,whichisbotheffectiveanddesirable,aslongaseachlayerisproperly secured [35]. By using a 2D matrix-based approach, it can be ensured to cover securitypractices ateachstageofthedevelopmentprocess. Thismatrixsupportsboth developers and cluster administrators to keep track of the security status for the whole cluster. It simplifies the complex structure of Kubernetes and its attack vector. That’s why this is more comprehensive and better than other frameworks.

Development Stage Deployment Stage Runtime Stage Cluster Layer Role-based Access Control Authentication and Authorization Secrets Management Namespace Boundaries Namespace Resource QuotasDynamic Admission ControlMonitoring, Alerting and Auditing Workload/Pod LayerStatic Analysis of YAML file and configuration Network Policies LimitRanger Resource Management for PodsPod Security Standards and Pod Security AdmissionPod Security Standards and Pod Security Admission, Deploy Honeypot Workload and Intrusion Detection Systems, Monitoring, Alerting and Auditing Resources LayersCIS Security Benchmark Container LayerBuild Secure Image Image Scanning in development phase LimitRangerImage Scanning in deployment phaseImage Scanning at the runtime stage Container Threat Analysis Table 3: The proposed matrix of Kubernetes security 4.2 Matrix Design TheKubernetesmatrixisdesignedin2dimensions. Thecolumnsincludedifferent stagesofasoftwaredevelopmentprocess. Inthisminimalandintroductoryversion, there are 3 phases defined, including Development Stage,Deployment Stage,Runtime Stage. These are inspired by the DevOps life cycle and closely related to this cycle in 2.3. On the other hand, the rows cover different layers of a Kubernetes cluster, including Cluster Layer,Workload Layer,Resources Layer,Container Layer.

4.2.1 Layer Definition From the lowest layer, the container layer focuses on the fundamental building block ofKubernetes’sworkload,whichistheDockerimageandtheDockercontainer. At thislayer, securitypracticesareappliedtomakesureattackvectorsarecutdownfrom thecontainersrunningapplicationservices. ThisincludesconfigurationsofDocker images and libraries installed in the container environment. Resource Layer represents resources running inside a Kubernetes cluster. This layerfocusesonpracticesthatprotectKubernetesresourcesinthecontrolplaneand workernodes. TheresourcesinthecontrolplanearetheAPIserver,etcdkey-value database, and scheduler. The worker node includes kubelet and kubeproxy. These componentsarethecoreofKubernetesarchitectureandhavebeenillustratedinsection 2.2.1. Eachcomponentisreallyimportant,andtheyhavedifferentspecialpermissions

tooperatetheentirecluster. Nodecomponentsmaintainpodsandapplicationsrunning, while the control plane controls the whole cluster. In this layer, security is assured by applying CIS Benchmarks, a set of configurations. WorkloadLayerincludespracticesthatworkforagroupofapplicationsrunning business logic inside pods. The main practices in this layer harden the pods and applicationswithstaticscanningtodetectvulnerabilities. Inaddition,theconfiguration covers resource management. The highest layer, which is the Cluster Layer, comprises protections for the whole system at the cluster range and configuration impact on different namespaces. The practices for this layer such as RBAC, secrets management, and namespace resource quotas, have the highest impact scope. 4.2.2 Stage Definition The proposed matrix is inspired by the DevOps life cycle at 2.3 and organizes Kubernetes security practices into three core stages. These stages provide a structured approachtosecuringKubernetesenvironmentswhileallowingflexibilityforadaptation based on project-specific workflows. •Build stage: software is designed with requirements and developed. •Deployment Stage: software is built into the artifact and deployed. •Runtime Stage: container and cluster are running and maintained in the cluster. Whilethese3stagesprovidefunctionalstructure,thedevelopmentteammayadjust the matrix to fix their need.

There are possible added stages for a workflow: •Pre-Build Stage: A team can integrate preliminary steps for security, such as threatmodellingandsecurityplanning,anddocumentpreparationforsuitable cloud providers. •Sub-stages before release: Before the final release, there can be multiple stages for testing in a pre-production environment. In this stage, practices can be applied to validate network policies and fine-tune RBAC rules before releasing to production. •Termination Stage: A cluster can include many jobs and cron jobs that request persistent volumes during task running. This supports cleaning up processes to clear data remains and frees up space. These variations accommodate diverse workflows, such as legacy system integrations or multi-cloud deployments, without compromising security. While DevSecOps pipelines automate security across the software development lifecycle, this framework differs in scope and flexibility. This matrix allows dev teams to choose solutions, security tools, and implementations that best fit their environment.

4.2.3 Item Definition The proposed Kubernetes security matrix provides a systematic framework for addressing risks across the entire lifecycle of a Kubernetes environment. Each cell in the matrix contains multiple security measures designed for specific Kubernetes components, such as the control plane, pods/workloads, or containers, at a defined stage of development. Arrangement of items is presented in Table 3. Each item in the matrix is described in detail in section 3. The item reference would describe in detail instructions on how to conduct the practice. This approach not only providesahigh-levelblueprintbutalsoprovidesdetailedinstructionstoimplementthe configuration. In short, the matrix provides a high-level, adaptable blueprint for securing Kubernetes. Each cell references detailed instructions for engineers, such as configuring Pod Security Admission (PSA) for Workloads, integrating Trivy into image scanning, etc. Thisend-to-endmitigationdemonstrateshowthematrixensuresrisksarefully addressed.

4.3 How to Apply When Building And Releasing Software The matrix serves as both a strategic guide and a practical checklist for securing Kubernetes environments. By aligning security practices with software development stagesandarchitecturallayers,developerteamscansystematicallymitigateriskswhile maintaining agility. However, it can be challenging to apply this approach to a real software engineer’sstandardprocess. In real-life scenarios, developerteamsworkin a linear order with tasks followed by the next tasks. There must be a method to convert the 2D approach into a linear order of configuration. This can possibly be done by applying the two methods below: layer by layer and stage by stage. 4.3.1 Layer By Layer Inalayer-by-layerimplementation,secureconfigurationsareappliedandtestedfor each layer. In a simple term, the security practices are conducted from the bottom to the upper layers. First, an application with multiple services and clusters can be built, deployed, and run. Second, image and container hardening are involved to ensure security at the first layer for containers.

Then, configuration for middle layers, which are components in the worker nodes, pods, and the control plane, is implemented. Finally, the system is tested with practice from the top layer with a configuration correlated to the whole cluster’s impacts. It is not mandatory to configure layers in the above order. Each layer target is isolated,andtheconfigurationineachlayerdoesnotimpacttheothers. However,after each layer configuration, to make sure the functional requirements of the project work rationallywithoutbrokenfeatures,thewholeapplicationflowshouldbeend-to-end tested. This method requires many iterations of configuration and testing, but it is morefriendlyadaptedandconducted. Anyinterruptedsettingupcouldbeeasilyfound and fixed.

4.3.2 Stage By Stage Different from the layer-by-layer implementation, the stage-by-stage method requires teams to come up with the configuration across the whole cluster. This is slightly similar to the Waterfall Model, a traditional software development method introduced in 1970. All requirements and configurations are analyzed and defined carefully. This makes the matrix implementation seem to be short for a project, but the workload for each stage is enormous, especially the Development Stage. Moreover, it is extremely complicated to troubleshoot errors. 4.3.3 Agile Implementation Compare the two implementations above; each has its pros and cons. In modern software development philosophy, iterative and incremental approaches are foundational. Iterative elements allow the developer team to refine features based on feedback, and incremental elements ensure stable progress. Incremental principles support the team in releasing the features quickly. It is now common for organizations to release prototypes or minimum viable products (MVPs) rapidlytogatherfeedback. Fromthefeedback,featuresareupdated,upgraded,and addediteratively. CombinedwithDevOps,newfeaturesarecontinuouslyintegrated and delivered (CI/CD).

To adapt to modern software development methodologies and tomakeexpandingthesoftwarewithnewfeaturessmooth,aniterativeapproach,which islayerbylayer,canbeappliedtotheproposedmatrix. Asimplewaytoexecutethe matrix for an initial insecure cluster is presented in section 4.3.1. Furthermore, when new features or services are added later, multiple iterations, which means multiple layers, can be applied to the new features. This makes the product delivered smoothly to end users without much interruption to the experience. This approach will be performed in the next section with a real case study implementation.

Figure 6: ARA2 architecture 5 Case Study And Result Inthissection,acasestudyapplicationisimplementedtovalidatetheeffectiveness ofamatrix-basedsecurityapproachforhardeningaKubernetescluster,addressing risks at the container, node, Pod, and cluster levels. The application overview and clusterenvironmentarepresentedinsection5.1. Section5.2willdescribeindetail the configuration ofcontainer andimage security. Section 5.3 discusses thepractice of hardening components in the control plane and the worker node. Section 5.4 shows configurations for Pod, which include higher-level workloads such as StatefulSet, Job andDeployment. Finally,section5.5examinespracticesforcluster-wideconfiguration. 5.1 Application Overview Anonymous Registration Anonymous Access Protocol (ARA2) is a distributed authenticationsystemdesignedforKubernetesenvironments. Thissystemisacryptographic flowthatallowsuserstoauthenticateusingcryptographictokenexchangesbetween decentralisedcomponentsandthenaccessprotectedresources. TheARA2application serves as an ideal examination for the security matrix due to its multi-component architecture, sensitive data and complex multiple operations. This system consists of the main custom services: Dealer,Guard,Access,User,Wikiand 2 dependencies: Strimzi Kafka Operator andMongoDB. Figure 6 show the architecture of the case study system. All services in ARA2 are deployed across multiple instancesto create a distributed environment. All user requests are HTTP-based, and all connections between services in the cluster are made through the Strimzi Kafka Operator. The general workflow of the system can be simplified into three main processes: •System setup: Dealers generate a token and distribute it to Guards. Guards then generate a token from the received data.

•User Authentication: User identifies using the generated token to the Dealer via the User proxy. •User Authorisation: User accesses through the Wiki service using a user token andanaccesskey. Thecombination issenttotheGuardtovalidatepermissions 5.1.1 Dealer The Dealer service is the first crucial component in the protocol. It provides the access key token to the user. Given the requirement of anonymous and distributed authentication,theDealermustremainaseparatecomponent,ensuringitcannotobtain more information than necessary to provide the access key. Additionally, since the Dealer’sdegreemustremainsecret,itoperatesasamulti-instancecomponent, with each instance holding its own degree. The Dealer’s main purpose in the setup stage is to generate its own polynomial, partition it into N shares and send it to the Guard. Duringtheauthenticationstage,theDealermustverifytheidentificationcredentials and reply with its token.

The life cycle of a Dealer comprises the following steps: •Connect to the Kafka broker •Subscribe to its topic •Request for the topics of the Guards from the Kafka broker •Check for the existence of a state file (which indicates a previously aborted execution). •If the state file does not include the value of its polynomial or the state file does not exist, generate its own polynomial and save it in the state file. •If the state file does not indicate that the token was already sent to the Guards, partition the token and send it to the Guards. •Continuously formessages from the AuthManager, including the user’s identification, and process the messages accordingly 5.1.2 Guard TheGuardcomponentiscrucialtotheimplementationoftheprotocolasitverifies user accessto the resource. Itfunctions as the Guardin the original protocol. Due to the requirement for anonymous and distributed authentication, the Guard must remain aseparatecomponent. Itshouldnotacquiremoreinformationthannecessarytoverify userauthorization. Additionally,sincetheGuard’sowncomputeddegreemustremain secret,itisamulti-instancecomponent,witheachinstancemaintainingitsowndegree. The Guard’s two main functions are exchanging messages with Dealers during the setupphasetocreateitsownkeyandusethatkeytoverifyusers’requeststoresources. The life cycle of a Guard consists of the following steps:

•Connect to the Kafka broker •Subscribe to Kafka topic •Checkfortheexistenceofastatefile(indicatingapreviouslyabortedexecution) •Ifthestatefileincludesitsowntoken,setitscurrenttokentotheonestatedin the file •Continuously listen for messages from the Dealers containing their partitioned tokens, add them to its current token, and update the state file •ContinuouslylistenformessagesfromtheAccesscontainingthetokensofusers attempting to access the resource 5.1.3 User This service communicates with the Dealers through the Kafka queues. The main function of the User service is to authenticate users by forwarding messages between users and Dealers. This service is exposed as an HTTP API in the cluster. After users get the static authentication page from Access, they send a request to the User service API, and messages are finally forwarded to the Dealers. The life cycle of a User service consists of: •Connect to the Kafka broker. •Subscribe to Kafka topic •Expose HTTP API and forward requests to Dealer •Listen for Dealers’ replies and respond Dealer tokens to the user 5.1.4 Access Access service allows users to fetch the authentication page in the first stage of the flow.

Section: Hardening Application Deployment in Kubernetes > Page 47. AfterbeingauthenticatedbytheUserserviceandtheDealer. Usersaccessthe resourcesthroughAccessagain. TherequestwouldbeauthorisedbyaGuardatthis stage before going to the Wiki Service. The life cycle of Access includes: •Connect to Kafka broker •Subscribe to Kafka topic •Expose and HTTP API to serve an HTML page •ContinuouslylistenformessagesfromtheGuardsandstorethemuntilallofthe corresponding texts for a user are received.

5.1.5 Wiki Wikicomponentallowsuserstoperformread,create,andupdateoperationsondata whichisstoredinaMongoDBdatabase. Userscanonlyaccessthisserviceandrequest dataiftheyhavepermissions. PermissionsarekeysreceivedfromDealers,andthey arefirstverifiedbytheGuardcomponent. Aftertheauthorisation,theusercanthen access the persistent data in the cluster. 5.1.6 Environment Setup ARA2isdeployedintheremoteAaltodescribedintableHardwarespecifications,and the Kubernetes cluster is described in table Cluster specifications.
